variables.A: Amplitude — maximum displacement from mean position (m)
variables.T: Time period of one complete oscillation (s)
variables.k: Force constant of the restoring force (N/m)

locked_facts: Displacement x = A sin(ωt + φ) where A is amplitude, ω is angular frequency, φ is initial phase
Velocity v = Aω cos(ωt + φ) — maximum at mean position (v_max = Aω), zero at extremes
Acceleration a = −ω²x — always directed toward mean position, maximum at extremes (a_max = ω²A)
Restoring force F = −kx where k = mω² — force is proportional to displacement and opposite in direction
Time period T = 2π/ω = 2π√(m/k) — independent of amplitude
At mean position: v = max, a = 0, KE = max, PE = min. At extremes: v = 0, a = max, KE = 0, PE = max
truth_statements: SHM occurs when the restoring force on a body is directly proportional to displacement from equilibrium and directed toward the mean position
The acceleration in SHM is always directed toward the mean position and its magnitude is proportional to the displacement
The time period and frequency of SHM are independent of amplitude — a defining property of SHM
Velocity and acceleration are 90° out of phase: when velocity is maximum, acceleration is zero, and vice versa
Any periodic motion that satisfies a = −ω²x is simple harmonic motion by definition

aha_moment.what_to_show: A particle oscillating with two different amplitudes side by side — both complete one cycle in exactly the same time. Period counters above each show identical T values even though one swings much wider.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Both particles reach their extremes and return to the mean position simultaneously despite very different amplitudes — the period readouts are identical
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students expect that a larger amplitude means the particle takes longer to return, but the increased speed at larger amplitudes exactly compensates for the larger distance

simulation_states.label: Setup
simulation_states.description: A particle on a horizontal line displaced to position x = A from the mean position (x = 0). Mean position marked clearly. Displacement x, amplitude A, and restoring force arrow F = −kx shown.
simulation_states.emphasis: Identify the mean position, the displacement, and the direction of the restoring force

simulation_states.label: Buildup
simulation_states.description: Particle oscillates back and forth. Vectors for displacement (green), velocity (blue), and acceleration (red) update in real time. A sinusoidal x-t graph traces simultaneously on the right side.
simulation_states.emphasis: Velocity is maximum at mean position where acceleration is zero. Acceleration is maximum at extremes where velocity is zero. The x-t graph is a perfect sine curve.

simulation_states.label: Aha
simulation_states.description: Two particles appear — same spring constant, same mass — but one has amplitude A and the other has 2A. Both oscillate and complete each cycle at exactly the same time. Period counters show identical values.
simulation_states.emphasis: Despite one particle travelling twice the distance, both finish each cycle together. T = 2π√(m/k) has no amplitude term.

simulation_states.label: Explore
simulation_states.description: Interactive: adjust amplitude, mass, and spring constant. Observe how T changes with m and k but NOT with A. Real-time x-t, v-t, and a-t graphs update. Formula T = 2π√(m/k) displayed with live values.
simulation_states.emphasis: Drag the amplitude slider — period does not change. Change mass or spring constant — period changes. This proves amplitude independence.

common_misconceptions.belief: Larger amplitude means longer time period — the particle has to travel more distance so it takes more time
trigger_phrases: bada amplitude toh zyada time lagega
amplitude badhao toh period badh jaayega
common_misconceptions.correction: NCERT Ch. 13 states that the time period T = 2π√(m/k) is independent of amplitude. A particle with larger amplitude also has proportionally larger maximum velocity (v_max = Aω), so the extra distance is exactly compensated.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Two particles with different amplitudes oscillating in perfect synchrony — identical period readouts

common_misconceptions.belief: Acceleration is maximum at the mean position because the particle moves fastest there
trigger_phrases: acceleration sabse zyada mean pe hogi
common_misconceptions.correction: NCERT states a = −ω²x. At the mean position x = 0, so acceleration is zero. Maximum acceleration occurs at the extremes where x = ±A. High speed does not mean high acceleration — acceleration depends on how quickly velocity changes direction.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Freeze at mean position showing velocity arrow at maximum but acceleration arrow vanishing to zero, then freeze at extreme showing velocity zero but acceleration arrow at maximum
